category_summary:
Insider industry intelligence dominated today's AI discourse. **Allie K Miller** [shared 12 detailed reflections](/?date=2026-03-21&category=social#item-882f40c9d932) from separate meetings with **Anthropic**, **OpenAI**, and **Google**, highlighting builders as an underserved cohort and the **OpenClaw** inflection point. **MIT Technology Review** [published an exclusive interview](/?date=2026-03-21&category=social#item-e433aff9cfcf) with **OpenAI** chief scientist **Jakub Pachocki** on the firm's new grand challenge.

- **Clement Delangue** [confirmed **Cursor's** new model](/?date=2026-03-21&category=social#item-70e260e95e2a) is based on **Kimi** (Moonshot AI), sparking major discussion about Chinese open-source models powering Western products. Meanwhile, **Ethan Mollick** noted **Alibaba's Qwen** and **Xiaomi** appear to be [steering away from open weights](/?date=2026-03-21&category=social#item-cdb5d67d5fef) — a potential turning point for open-source AI.
- **François Chollet** [announced **ARC-AGI-3**](/?date=2026-03-21&category=social#item-90eff37a5e72) launching next week and separately [critiqued dismissive discourse](/?date=2026-03-21&category=social#item-01860e774cdc) patterns when AI systems fail at tasks.
- **Google AI** recapped a busy week of launches including vibe coding in **AI Studio** and the **Stitch** design canvas. **Mollick** observed that the Big Three labs [risk converging](/?date=2026-03-21&category=social#item-88ce920fa624) on identical coding-tool UX while **Google** quietly experiments with more diverse, unconventional approaches.
- **Andrew Gordon Wilson** (NYU) [delivered a pointed critique](/?date=2026-03-21&category=social#item-5c0b565c1dda) of a new generation of deep learning researchers chasing trends without building foundational understanding. At **NVIDIA GTC**, **Scobleizer** [reported autonomous vehicles](/?date=2026-03-21&category=social#item-a92bb8ff57b1) from multiple companies are evolving so fast that human driving may soon feel outdated.

items.title:
Yesterday, I met with Anthropic and OpenAI and Google. 

(Separately, of course.)

And while the con...
items.content:
Yesterday, I met with Anthropic and OpenAI and Google. 

(Separately, of course.)

And while the conversations were largely confidential, I do want to share some aggregated reflections on the day as well as general SF takeaways. 

⬇️ 

1) Competitive advantage as a solo practitioner really does come from taking action and finding an area with a bit of friction and doubling down. Ex: memory management right now isn’t perfect, but allocating an hour to improving that system gives you a ton of leverage over others 

2) SF continues to be the number one place for AI work. I know that’s not surprising. I would put New York at a healthy second place. SF tends to be more about crazy agent experiments for the thrill of capability and discovery and NYC tends to be more about kinda crazy agent experiments to find new ways to make money. Not saying either is better. But I met several people renting two apartments to straddle these worlds. You want the frontier of SF and enterprise insights of NYC. It’s one reason I travel between them so much. 

3) All AI labs want to hear more from people. All of them. What are you using it for, what do you like, what do you hate, what do you need. Users have a TON of power on the direction of these tools. Keep testing and tweeting at them!!

4) There is very clearly a third customer cohort that is bubbling and underserved. It’s not developers…it’s not the business professional basic users…it’s builders. Everyone can build now. It’s marketing and sales folks vibe coding. It’s legal folks building complex skills. It’s a finance expert building a side project. This is a really undertapped customer base. They feel the Cursors of the world are too complex and doc summarization tools of the world are too basic. 

5) Not sure if it was just sample size, but far fewer people were wearing tech gear compared to when I lived in SF. Everyone was still dressed casually, but I used to see Splunk and Optimizely and Slack and VC gear everywhere. People seem more in stealth swag now. 

6) We may soon have our world model moment. 

7) Speed of iteration and shipping is faster than I’ve ever seen. We see the nonstop drops from Anthropic. We see that because of scale, providers can get a much faster feedback loop of products or features that aren’t hitting. A lot of 2025 was experimentation, but ever since the OpenClaw moment over the holidays, the releases from all three labs have been more concentrated on…things that sorta look and feel like OpenClaw. 

8) Small teams can pull off more than ever before. Small teams are the powerhouses of innovation right now. This means that finding new ways to share knowledge, break silos, and remove duplicate work is going to be even more important. AI agents functioning as actually teammates that support an entire system is key. 

9) Build more Skills. Build better Skills. 

10) Misinformation on AI tools and leaks spread FAST. I’ve seen so many fake stories on these AI labs. Your company needs to actually TEST these tools on your actual use cases to know which models and tools are best and you need to not make large-scale snap decisions based on a rumor of a rumor of a rumor. We will see more volatility. Plan for it. 

11) You can feel the seriousness of this moment. Even during random conversations I had in line at a cafe. Lots of folks worried about job loss and lack of meaning. 

12) Mac minis were sold out ;)

items.content_html:
Yesterday, I met with Anthropic and OpenAI and Google.

(Separately, of course.)

And while the conversations were largely confidential, I do want to share some aggregated reflections on the day as well as general SF takeaways.

⬇️

1) Competitive advantage as a solo practitioner really does come from taking action and finding an area with a bit of friction and doubling down. Ex: memory management right now isn’t perfect, but allocating an hour to improving that system gives you a ton of leverage over others

2) SF continues to be the number one place for AI work. I know that’s not surprising. I would put New York at a healthy second place. SF tends to be more about crazy agent experiments for the thrill of capability and discovery and NYC tends to be more about kinda crazy agent experiments to find new ways to make money. Not saying either is better. But I met several people renting two apartments to straddle these worlds. You want the frontier of SF and enterprise insights of NYC. It’s one reason I travel between them so much.

3) All AI labs want to hear more from people. All of them. What are you using it for, what do you like, what do you hate, what do you need. Users have a TON of power on the direction of these tools. Keep testing and tweeting at them!!

4) There is very clearly a third customer cohort that is bubbling and underserved. It’s not developers…it’s not the business professional basic users…it’s builders. Everyone can build now. It’s marketing and sales folks vibe coding. It’s legal folks building complex skills. It’s a finance expert building a side project. This is a really undertapped customer base. They feel the Cursors of the world are too complex and doc summarization tools of the world are too basic.

5) Not sure if it was just sample size, but far fewer people were wearing tech gear compared to when I lived in SF. Everyone was still dressed casually, but I used to see Splunk and Optimizely and Slack and VC gear everywhere. People seem more in stealth swag now.

6) We may soon have our world model moment.

7) Speed of iteration and shipping is faster than I’ve ever seen. We see the nonstop drops from Anthropic. We see that because of scale, providers can get a much faster feedback loop of products or features that aren’t hitting. A lot of 2025 was experimentation, but ever since the OpenClaw moment over the holidays, the releases from all three labs have been more concentrated on…things that sorta look and feel like OpenClaw.

8) Small teams can pull off more than ever before. Small teams are the powerhouses of innovation right now. This means that finding new ways to share knowledge, break silos, and remove duplicate work is going to be even more important. AI agents functioning as actually teammates that support an entire system is key.

9) Build more Skills. Build better Skills.

10) Misinformation on AI tools and leaks spread FAST. I’ve seen so many fake stories on these AI labs. Your company needs to actually TEST these tools on your actual use cases to know which models and tools are best and you need to not make large-scale snap decisions based on a rumor of a rumor of a rumor. We will see more volatility. Plan for it.

11) You can feel the seriousness of this moment. Even during random conversations I had in line at a cafe. Lots of folks worried about job loss and lack of meaning.

12) Mac minis were sold out ;)

Looks like it’s confirmed Cursor’s new model is based on Kimi! It reinforces a couple of things:
- o...
items.content:
Looks like it’s confirmed Cursor’s new model is based on Kimi! It reinforces a couple of things:
- open-source keeps being the greatest competition enabler
- another validation for chinese open-source that is now the biggest force shaping the global AI stack
- the frontier is no longer just about who trains from scratch, but who adapts, fine-tunes, and productizes fastest (seeing the same thing with OpenClaw for example).

items.title:
From vibe coding to vibe designing and beyond, it's been a busy week! Here’s what we launched:

—  A...
items.content:
From vibe coding to vibe designing and beyond, it's been a busy week! Here’s what we launched:

—  A new, full-stack vibe coding experience in @GoogleAIStudio that delivers a smarter agent, multiplayer and collaborative builds, secure login and storage, real-world services connections, and more

— @StitchbyGoogle from @GoogleLabs evolved into an AI-native design canvas that turns your natural language prompts into production-ready front-end code and UI/UX prototypes

— Upgraded Gemini API tooling that unlocks combining function calling with built-in tools (Google Search, etc.) in a single Gemini API call, along with @GoogleMaps support in all Gemini 3 models

— A no-cost, self-serve platform that lets anyone (educators, companies, local meetups) host their own public AI hackathons on @Kaggle

— Personal Intelligence expanded to more U.S. users for free across the @GeminiApp, Gemini in @GoogleChrome, and AI Mode in Google Search
items.author: @GoogleAI
items.summary: As covered in [Social](/?date=2026-03-20&category=social#item-e9d648c3be85) yesterday, Google AI summarizes a week of launches: full-stack vibe coding in AI Studio, Stitch design canvas for UI/UX, upgraded Gemini API tooling with function calling + built-in tools, Kaggle hackathon platform, and expanded Personal Intelligence for US users.
items.reasoning: Significant product roundup from Google AI with multiple notable launches. Vibe coding in AI Studio, Stitch evolution, and Gemini API upgrades are substantive. High engagement (439 likes, 31K views). Multiple concrete product announcements.

items.content_html:
From vibe coding to vibe designing and beyond, it's been a busy week! Here’s what we launched:

— A new, full-stack vibe coding experience in @GoogleAIStudio that delivers a smarter agent, multiplayer and collaborative builds, secure login and storage, real-world services connections, and more

— @StitchbyGoogle from @GoogleLabs evolved into an AI-native design canvas that turns your natural language prompts into production-ready front-end code and UI/UX prototypes

— Upgraded Gemini API tooling that unlocks combining function calling with built-in tools (Google Search, etc.) in a single Gemini API call, along with @GoogleMaps support in all Gemini 3 models

— A no-cost, self-serve platform that lets anyone (educators, companies, local meetups) host their own public AI hackathons on @Kaggle

— Personal Intelligence expanded to more U.S. users for free across the @GeminiApp, Gemini in @GoogleChrome, and AI Mode in Google Search

items.title: Just had a ride in the NVIDIA autonomous vehicle. I could argue that the Tesla is slightly smoother,...
items.content:
Just had a ride in the NVIDIA autonomous vehicle. I could argue that the Tesla is slightly smoother, but that is missing the point.

The point I learned this week by hanging out with a bunch of different companies building autonomous vehicles is that AI at a variety of different companies is evolving so quickly that within 18 months you will see a bunch of different ones shipping and moving from level two (gotta pay attention) to level four (no human needed).

Now the narrative will switch to one of scale.

Uber went from an idea in a Paris snow storm to an international company driving people who live in slums in South Africa in four years.

My expectation is that AVs will reach the slum in less time.

Will it be a USA based company like Tesla? 

Or a Chinese company that reaches the slum (hence meaning it will reach at least 25% of humans on earth in between) first?

We now will switch to one of convincing people AVs are a better way to get around than having humans drive. Regulation. Liability. Butts in seats. Experience. Price.

And to all the engineers who worked in these things for 20 years: thank you. 

Your efforts will save millions of lives so my kids won’t have to go to more funerals when they are my age.

Seven years ago @IrenaCronin and I wrote it is coming in our book.

It took a while but it is finally here.

Now the hard work of convincing every naysayer and hater is here.

items.title: There's a new generation of empirical deep learning researchers, hacking away at whatever seems tren...
items.content: There's a new generation of empirical deep learning researchers, hacking away at whatever seems trendy, blowing with the wind... no accumulation of real understanding, or foundations. No real passion or depth, just light amusement and career advancement. I'm hoping it's a phase.

items.title: There is some danger for the Big Three labs that they have run out of imagination and are now refini...
items.content:
There is some danger for the Big Three labs that they have run out of imagination and are now refining Codex/Claude Code/Antigravity, and building their next tools (Cowork, etc) to be similar.

These were good UX for AI's use & limits today, but not great UX for the future of AI.

items.title: When the latest AI systems can't do something, there's a category of people who will immediately say...
items.content:
When the latest AI systems can't do something, there's a category of people who will immediately say, "well humans can't do it either!" - Then they stop saying it when AI improves a bit.

Been hearing it for 4+ years, "humans can't reason either", "humans can't adapt to a task they haven't been prepared for", "humans can't follow instructions", "humans also suffer from hallucinations", etc. Until 2025 I was frequently told "humans can't do ARC 1 tasks either" (in reality any normally smart human would do >95% on ARC 1 if properly incentivized). Now that AI saturates ARC 1 they've completely stopped saying this.

items.content: In our last episode, careful tuning, scaling, and ensembles led to a 5x gain in data efficiency (requires 5x less data to get the same loss). Now, with a rephraser model, we can get an additional 1.8x gain in data efficiency. I know, everyone's compute constrained, but we're preparing for a data-constrained future.

Talked with @dee_bosa @CNBC about @nvidia and everything open-source AI! Some key points:

- Nvidia ...
items.content:
Talked with @dee_bosa @CNBC about @nvidia and everything open-source AI! Some key points:

- Nvidia is the new American open-source AI king
- 30% of fortune 500 are using Hugging Face and our goal is to get to the majority of them by the end of the year
- Agents will be much more open-source based than chatbots (ex OpenClaw)
- Agents empower all to train, fine-tune, and run their own models based on open-source
- We crossed 15M AI builders on HF and hope to have as much agents using the platform by the end of the year. Agents are the new users and customers of tech platforms

items.content_html:
Talked with @dee_bosa @CNBC about @nvidia and everything open-source AI! Some key points:

  • Nvidia is the new American open-source AI king
  • 30% of fortune 500 are using Hugging Face and our goal is to get to the majority of them by the end of the year
  • Agents will be much more open-source based than chatbots (ex OpenClaw)
  • Agents empower all to train, fine-tune, and run their own models based on open-source
  • We crossed 15M AI builders on HF and hope to have as much agents using the platform by the end of the year. Agents are the new users and customers of tech platforms

items.title:
The funny thing is, I'm writing more tests than ever since I've been writing more code with AI.

I n...
items.content:
The funny thing is, I'm writing more tests than ever since I've been writing more code with AI.

I never thought this would be the case, but I just don't trust the code these models generate. Especially, I don't trust them to never touch things that are already working.

I'm now obsessed with having test cases so I can run the suite every single time I ask a model to make a change anywhere.

items.title: My experience so far with LLM fiction writing is that it takes advantage of our assumption that an a...
items.content: My experience so far with LLM fiction writing is that it takes advantage of our assumption that an author is writing things for a reason, so we are charitable to a book's quirks & do mental work to assign them real meaning. But the AI doesn't have a reason for its quirks, its just bad writing.
items.author: @emollick.bsky.social
items.summary: Mollick observes that LLM fiction exploits readers' charitable assumption that an author writes things for a reason, but AI's quirks are just bad writing, not intentional craft.
items.reasoning: Highly engaged post (86 likes on Bluesky is significant). Deep observation about the cognitive asymmetry between human readers and LLM authors. Core insight about AI creative capabilities.
items.summary_html: Mollick observes that LLM fiction exploits readers' charitable assumption that an author writes things for a reason, but AI's quirks are just bad writing, not intentional craft.
items.content_html: My experience so far with LLM fiction writing is that it takes advantage of our assumption that an author is writing things for a reason, so we are charitable to a book's quirks & do mental work to assign them real meaning. But the AI doesn't have a reason for its quirks, its just bad writing.

items.title:
Every Pokémon Go player unknowingly trained future robots.

Over the years, players photographed 30 ...
items.content:
Every Pokémon Go player unknowingly trained future robots.

Over the years, players photographed 30 billion images from cities around the world.

And now that data is teaching delivery robots to navigate streets.

Niantic (company behind Pokémon Go), spun out an AI company called Niantic Spatial that's turning years of player data into a visual positioning system for machines.

When 500 million people installed the game and pointed their phones at buildings, landmarks, and street corners, they were also capturing detailed location data that normal GPS can't match:

> GPS signals bounce off skyscrapers and lose accuracy by up to 50 meters in dense cities.

> That's a dealbreaker for sidewalk delivery robots that need to stop at exactly the right point.

> Niantic Spatial trained a model on those 30 billion images to pinpoint a robot's exact location based on what its cameras see, not satellite signals.

> Delivery startup Coco Robotics is the first partner, using the system to guide thousands of robots across LA, Chicago, and Miami.

Don't think anyone thought they were training robots when they were chasing Pikachu during COVID.
items.author: @rowancheung
items.summary: Niantic (Pokémon Go maker) spun out Niantic Spatial, an AI company using 30 billion player-captured images to build visual positioning systems for delivery robots. Coco Robotics is deploying it in LA, Chicago, and Miami.
items.reasoning: Interesting story about data flywheel from gaming to robotics. Novel angle on how consumer data enables autonomous navigation. Good engagement (91 likes, 22K views). Though from a newsletter curator rather than primary source.
items.summary_html: Niantic (Pokémon Go maker) spun out Niantic Spatial, an AI company using 30 billion player-captured images to build visual positioning systems for delivery robots. Coco Robotics is deploying it in LA, Chicago, and Miami.

items.title: If you’ve ever had to deal with bureaucracy of any kind you’d realize how far far away we are from e...
items.content:
If you’ve ever had to deal with bureaucracy of any kind you’d realize how far far away we are from even having the basic technologically competent institutions that operate anywhere near the logical/deterministic bare minimum. AI systems will not change that. If AI is to have a TRULY transformative impact on human society, we we will either have to

1) fix and upgrade those processes (a massive undertaking)

OR (far more likely)

2) have the AI work around them (essentially handing over all the power to the AI systems)
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz argues that bureaucratic institutions are so far from technological competence that AI won't easily transform them. Offers two paths: fix/upgrade processes (massive undertaking) or have AI work around them (effectively handing power to AI). High engagement (5.7K views, 92 likes).
items.reasoning: Thoughtful, original analysis of AI's real-world impact limitations due to institutional inertia. The framing of the two paths (fix institutions vs. AI workaround) is an important strategic insight. High engagement and from a credible VC voice. Directly relevant to AI deployment and societal impact.

Offers two paths: fix/upgrade processes (massive undertaking) or have AI work around them (effectively handing power to AI). High engagement (5.7K views, 92 likes).

items.title:
She has one of the best seats on the future that exists.

Sydney Sykes runs the @NVIDIA Inception pr...
items.content:
She has one of the best seats on the future that exists.

Sydney Sykes runs the @NVIDIA Inception program's venture relationships. She works with all the other VCs investing in AI startups.

After hearing about what Inception is (it helps accelerate the many startups in the NVIDIA ecosystem) we dive into what is going on because of what I see as a major inflection point that AI just brought. You can feel it. The AI world has sped up in the past few weeks.

Worthy of watching, but I know you are busy with your own agents. Click the Grok button and ask it "please give me a lengthy report on what you learned by watching this."

I won't be offended. I do the same now. AI has greatly increased the demands on my life. So I don't have time to watch nearly as many videos as I once did. Who said AI would make us all jobless? They are wrong.

Thanks to CNQQ, the China Tech ETF, for having me report for its audience, and to NVIDIA for inviting me to do this work. And thank you to NVIDIA's conference team for arranging this and recording it.

Such an awesome way to start the day here on day four of @nvidiagtc.

Oh, and she asked to keep the investor report that my AI generated early this morning (posted it here then, it's quite good). Thanks @blevlabs for preparing me.

items.title: So we take AI's weird metaphors and, because we are good readers, assume they must be there for a re...
items.content: So we take AI's weird metaphors and, because we are good readers, assume they must be there for a reason, so we figure out how to make them meaningful in our heads. But they aren't meaningful, they are nonsense, and the AI is just stealing our processing power to cover its flaws.
items.author: @emollick.bsky.social
items.summary: Mollick argues that AI's weird metaphors trick readers into doing cognitive work to assign meaning that doesn't exist - AI is 'stealing our processing power to cover its flaws.'
items.reasoning: Insightful cognitive science perspective on human-AI interaction. The 'stealing processing power' framing is a novel and provocative way to describe the reader-AI dynamic.
items.summary_html: Mollick argues that AI's weird metaphors trick readers into doing cognitive work to assign meaning that doesn't exist - AI is 'stealing our processing power to cover its flaws.'
items.content_html: So we take AI's weird metaphors and, because we are good readers, assume they must be there for a reason, so we figure out how to make them meaningful in our heads. But they aren't meaningful, they are nonsense, and the AI is just stealing our processing power to cover its flaws.

items.title:
ByteDance just gave each agent its own computer. 

DeerFlow is a new open-source agent framework. It...
items.content:
ByteDance just gave each agent its own computer. 

DeerFlow is a new open-source agent framework. It orchestrates multiple AI agents with memory, sandboxes, and modular skills. 

> Researches, codes, and builds websites
> Runs bash commands inside Docker
> Spawns parallel sub-agents per task
> Remembers you across sessions 

One prompt triggers a chain. A lead agent breaks the task. Sub-agents run in parallel. Results merge. You get a complete deliverable. Skills load only when needed, keeping context windows lean. 

Until now, multi-agent systems either shared one messy context or required brittle orchestration code. 

DeerFlow treats agents like workers: isolated, specialized, and reporting back structured results to a lead agent that synthesizes everything. 

It works with GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, DeepSeek, or any OpenAI-compatible API. 

MIT license. 100% open-source.

items.title: LiteParse is our free, blazing-fast document parser that you can plug into 46+ different agents - wi...
items.content:
LiteParse is our free, blazing-fast document parser that you can plug into 46+ different agents - with one command 🔥

From Claude Code to OpenClaw to Cursor to Warp. Use liteparse to solve a task directly or read docs as context to write code.

All you have to do is `npx skills add run-llama/llamaparse-agent-skills --skill liteparse` (we borrowed Vercel’s format)

Check out our video below on using liteparse with Claude Code!

Repo: https://t.co/JNER0mVcB8
Blog: https://t.co/qmpDwlkidZ

items.title: CS theory conferences used to be single blind, though recently (in the last 5-10 years) they moved t...
items.content:
CS theory conferences used to be single blind, though recently (in the last 5-10 years) they moved to lightweight double blind. 1 benefit of single blind: authors can't submit trash without taking a reputational hit.

It's increasingly clear that paper submission can't be "free."
items.author: @thegautamkamath
items.summary: Gautam Kamath discusses how CS theory conferences moved from single to double blind review, arguing that paper submission can't be 'free' since double blind removes reputational accountability for low-quality submissions.
items.reasoning: Relevant academic process discussion from a CS researcher, moderate engagement. Touches on the broader issue of AI paper quality/volume but isn't directly about a new development.
items.summary_html: Gautam Kamath discusses how CS theory conferences moved from single to double blind review, arguing that paper submission can't be 'free' since double blind removes reputational accountability for low-quality submissions.

It's increasingly clear that paper submission can't be "free."

items.title: It wouldn’t make sense to throw giant blobs into a single context even if there were a 1B window. Su...
items.content: It wouldn’t make sense to throw giant blobs into a single context even if there were a 1B window. Sub-agents can grind through large data tasks and return just their results to a main thread without cluttering up context. It’s early days for this architecture but we’re already doing this with e.g. Alyx search.
items.author: @aparnadhinak
items.summary: Aparna Dhinakaran discusses sub-agent architecture for large data tasks - argues even with a 1B context window, using sub-agents that process data and return results is better than stuffing everything into one context. Mentions this is already happening with 'Alyx search'.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful technical insight about agentic architecture vs. large context windows from an AI industry figure. The sub-agent pattern is an important emerging paradigm.
items.summary_html: Aparna Dhinakaran discusses sub-agent architecture for large data tasks - argues even with a 1B context window, using sub-agents that process data and return results is better than stuffing everything into one context. Mentions this is already happening with 'Alyx search'.
items.content_html: It wouldn’t make sense to throw giant blobs into a single context even if there were a 1B window. Sub-agents can grind through large data tasks and return just their results to a main thread without cluttering up context. It’s early days for this architecture but we’re already doing this with e.g. Alyx search.

items.content: Reading the replies to the post, it is clear nobody actually tried to read this. But, to be clear, writing is a hard problem! And they really did get further with a plot arc than most LLM writing. But LLMs are bad judges of writing so the self-improvement loop fails pretty fast.

items.title: right now, we manage multiple agents but as they get better, does the capacity of one agent surpass ...
items.content:
right now, we manage multiple agents but as they get better, does the capacity of one agent surpass one person’s cognitive capacity to track and manage?

put differently, will we get to a point where we need teams of people managing a single agent?

just random food for thought

items.title: @SigGravitas for assistive cases, claude code/cowork offload that decision making to the agent harne...
items.content:
@SigGravitas for assistive cases, claude code/cowork offload that decision making to the agent harness. given a task over a set of pdfs, it'll use pypdf, look at the text, and if it's bad fallback to writing code for screenshotting

for batch cases, i think you can write simple heuristics to see if there are images in the document. liteparse is optimized for text not images (though it has paddleOCR as a VLM backbone if you want)
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex) discusses document parsing strategies - Claude Code uses pypdf with fallback to screenshot-based parsing for bad text, and mentions LiteParse's text vs image handling with PaddleOCR as VLM backbone.
items.reasoning: Technical detail from LlamaIndex founder about practical document parsing strategies in AI agent workflows. Useful for developers building document processing pipelines.
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex) discusses document parsing strategies - Claude Code uses pypdf with fallback to screenshot-based parsing for bad text, and mentions LiteParse's text vs image handling with PaddleOCR as VLM backbone.
items.content_html:
@SigGravitas for assistive cases, claude code/cowork offload that decision making to the agent harness. given a task over a set of pdfs, it'll use pypdf, look at the text, and if it's bad fallback to writing code for screenshotting

for batch cases, i think you can write simple heuristics to see if there are images in the document. liteparse is optimized for text not images (though it has paddleOCR as a VLM backbone if you want)

items.title:
and i don’t mean anytime in the short term

i was actually wondering if we’ll reach a point where mo...
items.content:
and i don’t mean anytime in the short term

i was actually wondering if we’ll reach a point where models get “good enough” that we don’t need to improve the model anymore - which led me to the question of what that would look like

this led me to imagining a point where the mod was maximally useful for one person, so naturally the need for a better model becomes one where getting the benefit out of it requires more than one person

all very hypothetical and fuzzy
items.author: @yoheinakajima
items.summary: Yohei Nakajima explores the idea of models getting 'good enough' that improvement stops, hypothesizing that at some point a model maximally useful for one person would need teams to extract further value.
items.reasoning: Thought-provoking speculation about AI capability saturation from BabyAGI creator. The idea of needing teams to manage single agents inverts current multi-agent paradigms. Moderate engagement.

items.content:
@_akitov In theory, you can customize the prompt I gave at the bottom of the article is to have it ask each subagent to make a plan before actually doing any implementation work.

The orchestrator can then review that plan before asking the subagent to proceed.

This is an especially good idea for more complex tasks that will be handed off to subagents.

You could even prompt it to evaluate the difficulty of a given task, and if it's extremely difficult, have it plan, and if not, take the standard approach.
items.author: @mattshumer_
items.summary: Matt Shumer discusses advanced coding agent orchestration patterns - having sub-agents plan before implementation, with the orchestrator reviewing plans, and dynamically adjusting approach based on task difficulty.
items.reasoning: Useful follow-up detail on agent orchestration but low engagement and is a reply to a specific question, not a standalone insight.
items.summary_html: Matt Shumer discusses advanced coding agent orchestration patterns - having sub-agents plan before implementation, with the orchestrator reviewing plans, and dynamically adjusting approach based on task difficulty.

@_akitov In theory, you can customize the prompt I gave at the bottom of the article is to have it ask each subagent to make a plan before actually doing any implementation work.

The orchestrator can then review that plan before asking the subagent to proceed.

This is an especially good idea for more complex tasks that will be handed off to subagents.

You could even prompt it to evaluate the difficulty of a given task, and if it's extremely difficult, have it plan, and if not, take the standard approach.

items.title:
I will talk about this on @wholemars’s Subscriber-only space on Sunday.

Truth is my eight year-old ...
items.content:
I will talk about this on @wholemars’s Subscriber-only space on Sunday.

Truth is my eight year-old Tesla was smoother than the Mercedes running NVIDIA software.

But seeing how the @NVIDIA AI decomposes the road into all its parts shows me how it will handle all the weird stuff that happens on the road. And we’ll quickly get smoother. 

Tesla's advantage is the fleet. On Omar's Space on Sunday, I'm going to explain why the fleet is going to bring major advantages to Tesla.

Until a car company or a group of car companies can get enough vehicles on the road with an NVIDIA computer, they're not going to be able to compete on experience in the car.

items.title: Take a few minutes and watch the attached video interview from this week's #NVIDIAGTC premier global...
items.content:
Take a few minutes and watch the attached video interview from this week's #NVIDIAGTC premier global AI conference. @Cognizant discusses with @RavitJain the Cognizant partnership with @AWSCloud and what it means for today's AI Enterprise.

Here are the key items discussed in the interview:

1) The Cognizant+AWS team is delivering Computer Vision AI solutions with latest hardware advancements from NVIDIA (increased compute speeds that enable near real-time prediction) that significantly reduce latency from signals to actions.

2) A major development discussed in this video is a Retail AI application to reduce "shrink" and financial loss, particularly due to the recent retail industry shift toward self-checkout systems.

3) Transforming Retail with these real-time AI-powered Vision Technologies focuses on enterprise scale, not just point solutions for single use cases.

4) AWS brings their enterprise scale expertise, while Cognizant brings their engineering experience to these deployments.

5) The specific implementation discussed here is Cognizant's LookR (Computer Vision for Retail) that uses the Metropolis Intelligent Vision AI Platform for Smart Infrastructure in combination with the AWS enterprise capabilities.

Watch the 4-minute video to learn more.

#AWSPartner #TheRavitShow
items.author: @KirkDBorne
items.summary: Kirk Borne discusses Cognizant+AWS Computer Vision AI solutions presented at NVIDIA GTC, focusing on retail 'shrink' reduction using real-time CV at enterprise scale with NVIDIA Metropolis platform.
items.reasoning: Contains substantive technical details about real-world CV AI deployment in retail. Discusses specific architecture (Cognizant LookR + Metropolis + AWS). GTC-relevant. Though promotional, has meaningful technical substance.

items.title: @OracleofMusk I used to believe like you, until I got a look at their simulator and talked to their ...
items.content:
@OracleofMusk I used to believe like you, until I got a look at their simulator and talked to their AI teams. 

The AI decomposes the road into all of its representative parts. So if something random happens on the road ahead of you—like a semi-trailer overturning and spitting a bunch of dirt and rocks on the interstate in front of you—it knows how to deal with that. 

It knows how to deal with:
1. Ice
2. Fog
3. All the edge cases I could think of that I've had in my life

I no longer believe they're not going to get there. The simulator system that they have is so advanced, it's fucking mind-blowing.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer describes being deeply impressed by NVIDIA's autonomous driving simulator and AI road decomposition system, claiming it can handle ice, fog, and edge cases like overturned semi-trailers.
items.reasoning: First-hand GTC impressions of NVIDIA's autonomous driving simulator capabilities. Provides specific examples of edge case handling.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer describes being deeply impressed by NVIDIA's autonomous driving simulator and AI road decomposition system, claiming it can handle ice, fog, and edge cases like overturned semi-trailers.
items.content_html:
@OracleofMusk I used to believe like you, until I got a look at their simulator and talked to their AI teams.

The AI decomposes the road into all of its representative parts. So if something random happens on the road ahead of you—like a semi-trailer overturning and spitting a bunch of dirt and rocks on the interstate in front of you—it knows how to deal with that.

It knows how to deal with:

1. Ice

2. Fog

3. All the edge cases I could think of that I've had in my life

I no longer believe they're not going to get there. The simulator system that they have is so advanced, it's fucking mind-blowing.

items.title: @Ram422 The fleet is Tesla's advantage, and you are right to point that out. Having many Teslas arou...
items.content:
@Ram422 The fleet is Tesla's advantage, and you are right to point that out. Having many Teslas around you while you are being driven around means the system has more perfect information about the real world. 

That leads to several improvements:
1. Routes are going to be faster and better.
2. The reasoning is going to be better.
3. The information in the cabin is going to be better.
4. The overall experience of being in a Tesla is going to be better.

That is why I think Tesla will take a significant share. When people get in a Tesla, it is going to tell them more about what is going on in the city around them (up to the point of saying, "Hey, there is a two-hour line at the taco joint you want to go to. Do you want to go somewhere else, or do you still want to go there?"). It will be something like that.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer elaborates on Tesla's fleet advantage for autonomous driving - better routes, reasoning, in-cabin info, and contextual recommendations like restaurant wait times.
items.reasoning: Detailed argument about fleet data advantages in autonomous driving, with specific user experience examples.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer elaborates on Tesla's fleet advantage for autonomous driving - better routes, reasoning, in-cabin info, and contextual recommendations like restaurant wait times.
items.content_html:
@Ram422 The fleet is Tesla's advantage, and you are right to point that out. Having many Teslas around you while you are being driven around means the system has more perfect information about the real world.

That leads to several improvements:

1. Routes are going to be faster and better.

2. The reasoning is going to be better.

3. The information in the cabin is going to be better.

4. The overall experience of being in a Tesla is going to be better.

That is why I think Tesla will take a significant share. When people get in a Tesla, it is going to tell them more about what is going on in the city around them (up to the point of saying, "Hey, there is a two-hour line at the taco joint you want to go to. Do you want to go somewhere else, or do you still want to go there?"). It will be something like that.

Our team had a great week in San Jose at @NVIDIAGTC !

Cohere leaders and technical staff delivered talks across the event, covering hardware efficiency, enterprise-grade automation, and innovation across the stack.

Our CEO, @aidangomez, also joined the GTC live stage to discuss the exciting possibilities for enterprise customers, the barriers to deploying enterprise technology into global economies, and what surprised him most about the evolution of AI adoption. 

It was great connecting with our customers and partners, including @Dell, @Microsoft, @Oracle, and @CoreWeave, as well as the many other brilliant minds across the ecosystem who are shaping the exciting future of AI.

#NVIDIAGTC

items.title: When I taught a graduate course years ago on data science for scientists, I would ask my students to...
items.content:
When I taught a graduate course years ago on data science for scientists, I would ask my students to consider the question, "How many cows are there in the state of Texas?" This was about 20 years ago. I asked my students what they thought would happen if they typed that question into the most popular web search engine.

After some discussion, we converged on the reality that the search engine (at that period of time) would most likely return web pages containing the words "cows" "state" "Texas", but the web pages would very likely *not* answer our question. I then pointed out to them the huge gap and significant difference between information search (and retrieval) and semantic search (and inference).

Well, today, semantic search, inference, knowledge extraction, insights discovery, and predictive reasoning are available at the click of a few buttons. A new platform with all those characteristics—empowering prediction (especially prediction) and accelerating the future of semantic knowledge discovery with Agentic AI—is @miromind_ai found at https://t.co/JztsMFfArU

Follow these links to learn more and—more importantly—to try MiroMind's MiroThinker for yourself:

● Live Product : https://t.co/nzf7ydTXFt <-- start here!

● MiroThinker GitHub : https://t.co/Br1taz0Hpe

● MiroFlow Framework : https://t.co/PUDHU5D82p

● Hugging Face Models : https://t.co/F3cDlMYTaa

Note: I did ask MiroMind the question "How many cows are there in the state of Texas?", and it returned a very detailed, informative, and comprehensive response, with citations! I also asked it many other questions, including some predictive questions (about bitcoin, quantum computing, emerging technologies, human trips to Jupiter, etc.), and I learned a lot — not just simplistic answers to my questions, but detailed responses that included numerous citations to sources (plus very detailed analyses of those sources) in order to expose and explain MiroMind's logical workflow in deriving its responses. That's some amazing AI model explainability and transparency on full display!

You should really try @MiroMind_ai for yourself.

One last point: their model has the highest score on predictive accuracy among all current AI models.
items.author: @KirkDBorne
items.summary: Kirk Borne shares a lengthy promotional post about MiroMind AI platform, framing it through a teaching anecdote about the evolution from keyword search to semantic search and predictive AI. Claims MiroMind's model has the highest predictive accuracy among all current AI models.
items.reasoning: While the framing about the evolution from information retrieval to semantic search is interesting, this is essentially a promotional post for MiroMind AI. The claim about highest predictive accuracy is unsubstantiated. Moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: Kirk Borne shares a lengthy promotional post about MiroMind AI platform, framing it through a teaching anecdote about the evolution from keyword search to semantic search and predictive AI. Claims MiroMind's model has the highest predictive accuracy among all current AI models.

It has turned it into an arms race.

#ElasticPartner

UGC is turning into a programmable AI system.

Instead of “hire a creator,” it b...
items.content:
The shift is this:

UGC is turning into a programmable AI system.

Instead of “hire a creator,” it becomes:

→ pick an avatar + voice
→ upload a script
→ add product visuals
→ generate influencer-style videos in minutes

That’s not content creation.
That’s content manufacturing.

items.title: Had to go see Project Hail Mary right away (it's based on the book of Andy Weir, of also The Martian...
items.content:
Had to go see Project Hail Mary right away (it's based on the book of Andy Weir, of also The Martian fame). Both very pleased and relieved to say that 1) the movie sticks very close to the book in both content and tone and 2) is really well executed.

The book is one of my favorites when it comes to alien portrayals because a lot of thought was clearly given to the scientific details of an alternate biochemistry, evolutionary history, sensorium, psychology, language, tech tree, etc. It's different enough that it is highly creative and plausible, but also similar enough that you get a compelling story and one of the best bromances in fiction. Not to mention the other (single-cellular) aliens. I can count fictional portrayals of aliens of this depth on one hand. A lot of these aspects are briefly featured - if you read the book you'll spot them but if you haven't, the movie can't spend the time to do them justice.

I'll say that the movie inches a little too much into the superhero movie tropes with the pacing, the quips, the Bathos and such for my taste, and we get a little bit less the grand of Interstellar and a little bit less of the science of The Martian, but I think it's ok considering the tone of the original content. And it does really well where it counts - on Rocky and the bromance. Thank you to the film crew for the gem!

Not to mention the other (single-cellular) aliens. I can count fictional portrayals of aliens of this depth on one hand. A lot of these aspects are briefly featured - if you read the book you'll spot them but if you haven't, the movie can't spend the time to do them justice.

I'll say that the movie inches a little too much into the superhero movie tropes with the pacing, the quips, the Bathos and such for my taste, and we get a little bit less the grand of Interstellar and a little bit less of the science of The Martian, but I think it's ok considering the tone of the original content. And it does really well where it counts - on Rocky and the bromance. Thank you to the film crew for the gem!

This is not about more alerts.

It’s about speed of analysis.

It’s speed.

When you can generate unlimited variations, you ...
items.content:
And the real advantage isn’t realism.

It’s speed.

When you can generate unlimited variations, you can:

→ A/B test hooks daily
→ iterate messaging fast
→ deploy across TikTok, Meta, Instagram, YouTube
→ stop guessing what converts

This is performance marketing getting its factory line.

items.title:
UGC works, but it has a scaling problem:

→ Creators are expensive
→ Filming takes weeks
→ Testing i...
items.content:
UGC works, but it has a scaling problem:

→ Creators are expensive
→ Filming takes weeks
→ Testing is slow
→ You get 3 versions when you need 300

The bottleneck isn’t creativity.

It’s production throughput.

UGC works, but it has a scaling problem:

→ Creators are expensive

→ You get 3 versions when you need 300

The bottleneck isn’t creativity.

It’s production throughput.

items.title: Roger Penrose, @RogerPenrose5, PhD, The Nobel Prize winner of 2020. The author of the influential bo...
items.content:
Roger Penrose, @RogerPenrose5, PhD, The Nobel Prize winner of 2020. The author of the influential book The Road to Reality, has offered profound insights into the foundations and limitations of Einstein's equations.

Tensor geometry provided Einstein general relativity with a language. Hereafter known as GR, yet quantum physics soon revealed its limitations. The smooth, continuous spacetime and local deterministic evolution of GR are fundamentally at odds with the discreteness, superposition, and entanglement inherent in quantum mechanics. As a result, GR remains incomplete when confronted with quantum phenomena.

Einstein's General Relativity is the ultimate classical physics theory with gravity as curved spacetime, described by elegant tensor equations, but push it to quantum scales, and it catastrophically breaks down. Singularities produce infinities, and spacetime loses its smooth geometry. GR directly contradicts quantum mechanics most sacred law. That information can never be destroyed. A century after Einstein, we still have no experimentally verified theory of quantum gravity from any lab.

Tensors enabled Einstein to encode gravity into the geometry of spacetime, but quantum theory soon revealed the cracks in this framework. GR treats spacetime as smooth, local, and classical, whereas quantum mechanics emphasizes Heisenberg’s principle, superposition, entanglement, and the possibility of quantized geometry.

Quantum Field Theory (QFT) excels at describing the quantum behavior of non gravitational force, while Einstein’s field equations remain unmatched in the classical description of the gravity domain. However, Einstein’s equations are not equipped to withstand the demands of the quantum frontier without significant modification.

Tensors let Einstein turn gravity into geometry. Roger Penrose helps show why that triumph is still not complete. GR assumes a smooth, classical spacetime, while quantum physics points to QFT, superposition, entanglement, and perhaps granular geometry itself. Einstein’s field equations were a masterpiece of classical physics but do not survive unchanged when applied to the quantum domain.

items.title:
💥Join @Commvault at RSAC 2026: https://t.co/EpyEZbdgYP 💥

Commvault's latest identity resilience off...
items.content:
💥Join @Commvault at RSAC 2026: https://t.co/EpyEZbdgYP 💥

Commvault's latest identity resilience offerings take center stage at this year's RSAC Conference (Booth S-0634), March 23-26 in San Francisco.🌐

Show attendees can grab a ringside seat for the ResOps Rumble where resilience and operations join forces to deliver unified cyber recovery, identity resilience, and data security.

➡️Register today for ransomware recovery demos and sessions, expert insights on identity resilience and clean recovery, and the ultimate prize – unified resilience for your organization.⬅️

What you can expect from @Commvault at RSAC 2026:
🟣Product announcements & presentations
🟣Live Demos
🟣Meet the Commvault Executives
🟣Public Sector Day
🟣The ResOps Rumble
🟣Rumble After-Party
🟣Learn how unified resilience stands up to today’s biggest threats: Misconfig Misfit, the Agentic Anomaly, and the Identity Impostor.

#RSAC2026 @OneRSAC #PowerOfCommunity #Commvault #ResOpsRumble #ResOps #CyberResilience #IdentityResilience

items.title:
The shift is this:

UGC is turning into a programmable AI system.

Instead of “hire a creator,” it b...
items.content:
The shift is this:

UGC is turning into a programmable AI system.

Instead of “hire a creator,” it becomes:

→ pick an avatar + voice
→ upload a script
→ add product visuals
→ generate influencer-style videos in minutes

That’s not content creation.
That’s content manufacturing.

items.title:
99 Ranch seafood / meat quality is pretty suspect

I bought a catfish, a tilapia, and a crab, all li...
items.content:
99 Ranch seafood / meat quality is pretty suspect

I bought a catfish, a tilapia, and a crab, all live, killed at the counter --- so as fresh as can be

I came home and steamed them

all 3 were quite inflammatory, way more than anything I get from whole foods

also tried some hotpot meat from their freezer -- very inflammatory

I'm guessing it's the way these animals were farmed and fed

in any case I'm staying away from 99 Ranch now
items.author: @TheGregYang
items.summary: Greg Yang (Microsoft Research) shares opinions about 99 Ranch supermarket seafood quality being inflammatory
